data.title: Wall St climbs after steep declines as tariff concerns dampen sentiment
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes up: Dow 1.51%, S&P 500 1.92%, Nasdaq 2.37%
  • Consumer sentiment at 57.9 in March
  • Tesla up on report it is planning lower-cost Model Y in Shanghai
  • Bullion miners up as gold prices cross $3,000 mark

U.S. equities staged a modest recovery on Friday, after a turbulent week marked by a broad selloff as investors assessed the economic fallout of the Trump administration's chaotic trade policies, putting major indexes on track for weekly losses.

The technology sector , which bore the brunt of the selloff, spearheaded sectoral gains with a 2.8% rise.

An index tracking banks (.SPXBK) gained 2.9%, while chip stocks such as Nvidia and Broadcom advanced, aiding the broader chip index's 3% rise.

"These growth companies that have been selling off these highs, the reality is their valuations were high, but they're good companies and they're leading the AI revolution," said Brian Klimke, chief market strategist at Cetera Investment Management.

"Sometimes, a little selloff is good and they can provide a buying opportunity."

All three major indices were on track for weekly losses. The benchmark S&P 500 is set for its fourth consecutive week of declines, marking its longest losing streak in seven months.

The investment climate remains clouded in uncertainty due to Trump's inconsistent tariff strategy. Recent levies on metal imports elicited swift retaliatory actions from Canada and the European Union. The U.S. president has also alluded to the prospect of further reciprocal tariffs in early April.

Several brokerages also downgraded their ratings on U.S. stocks and numerous companies issued cautious forecasts, citing economic worries.

The S&P 500 plunged into correction territory and saw more than $4 trillion in market value evaporate. The tech-heavy Nasdaq had already breached correction thresholds the prior week.

The blue-chip Dow , about 8% shy of its recent record peak, is on the brink of its worst week in two years, should current losses persist.

At 11:58 a.m. ET the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 615.60 points, or 1.51%, to 41,429.17, the S&P 500 gained 106.20 points, or 1.92%, to 5,627.72 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 410.02 points, or 2.37%, to 17,713.03.

Consumer staples , often seen as faring better in times of uncertainty, was on track for its biggest one-week drop since May 2022 after it came in the crossfire of Trump's tariff threats.

Stocks dipped briefly earlier in the day after a University of Michigan survey revealed a sharp decline in consumer sentiment for March, with inflation fears mounting.

Investors flocked to safe-haven assets, propelling gold past the key $3,000 barrier for the first time in history.

U.S.-listed shares of bullion miners surged, with Barrick Gold advancing 1.6% and Sibanye Stillwater rising 1.9%.

Tesla added 3.4%. A report said the automaker would make a lower-cost version of its best-selling Model Y in Shanghai, aiming to regain ground lost during a price war in its second-largest market.

The U.S. Senate was on the verge of passing a stopgap spending bill to avert a partial government shutdown.

The central bank's policy decisions will be closely watched next week. Traders expect the U.S. Federal Reserve to maintain interest rates at its upcoming meeting, according to LSEG data.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 5.39-to-1 ratio on the NYSE, and by a 3.53-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted one new 52-week high and five new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 29 new highs and 126 new lows.

data.title: Week Ahead - March 17th
data.description:
In the United States, investors will focus on the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision, which comes along with economic and "dot plot" interest-rate projections.

Key US data include retail sales, industrial production, and housing market indicators, including housing starts, building permits, and existing home sales.

Globally, interest rate decisions are expected from Japan, China, the UK, Brazil, Switzerland, and Sweden.

Inflation data will be released for Canada and Japan, while China will report retail sales, industrial production, house price index, and fixed asset investments.

In Europe, the UK’s unemployment rate and GfK consumer confidence, Germany’s ZEW economic sentiment, and Euro Area consumer confidence will be closely watched.

Meanwhile, New Zealand’s GDP growth rate and Canada’s retail sales will also be in focus.

data.title: German Carmaker BMW Flags EUR1 Billion Costs Over Tariffs
data.description:
Chief Executive Officer Oliver Zipse said BMW is taking a "rather conservative" provision of 1 billion euros for the effect of tariffs on operations.

Executives of the German luxury carmaker also said Friday at an earnings call, according to a FactSet transcript, that they expect a mid-three-digit million-euro hit from different tariffs involving the US, China, Europe, Mexico and Canada.

data.title: SAP sued by tech company Celonis over business process software
data.description:
German technology company Celonis has sued Europe's largest software maker SAP in U.S. court for allegedly monopolizing part of the business software market.

In a lawsuit filed on Thursday in federal court in San Francisco, California, business software maker Celonis said SAP illegally restricts third-party software vendors' ability to access and use customer data, affecting billions of dollars of commerce.

SAP's software applications are widely used by companies for finance, sales, supply chain and other functions. Celonis makes “process mining” software that can help companies review processes in real-time.

Munich-based Celonis accused its larger German competitor of violating U.S. antitrust law and other provisions by making it harder and more expensive for Celonis to access client data stored in SAP’s applications. SAP makes a rival process-mining software program and wants to exclude Celonis from the market, according to the lawsuit.

SAP on Friday said it was reviewing the lawsuit but declined to comment further. Celonis declined to comment.

Celonis was founded in 2011 and now boasts a $13 billion valuation and more than 3,000 employees. The company’s process-mining software analyzes the “digital footprint” of computerized event logs that are stored within systems created by SAP and other providers.

In the lawsuit, Celonis said SAP had taken steps to prevent its customers from sharing data with vendors such as Celonis without paying costly fees.

SAP has “embarked upon an aggressive campaign to exclude third-party application and technology providers from its ecosystem,” according to the lawsuit.

The lawsuit also accused SAP of making false and misleading statements to customers about the potential harm of using Celonis software.

Celonis asked a judge to award unspecified monetary damages and to issue an order halting SAP's allegedly anticompetitive conduct.

The case is Celonis SE and Celonis Inc v. SAP SE and SAP America Inc, U.S. District Court, Northern District of California, No. 3:25-cv-02519.

For plaintiff: Jennifer Fleury, Justin Bernick and Michael Maddigan of Hogan Lovells

For defendant: No appearance yet

Read more:

SAP must face Teradata trade secrets and antitrust lawsuit, US court rules

data.title: Reddit Is Withstanding Google's AI Push — Market Talk
data.description: Reddit's relationship with Google appears to be expanding, despite some investors' perception that it's soured, Deutsche Bank analysts say. During a fireside chat with Deutsche, CEO Steve Huffman expressed confidence in Reddit's relationship with Google, the analysts say. The two companies are collaborating on AI and Google's interest in indexing Reddit's content. Huffman believes the inter-personal element of Reddit differentiates it from Google's AI-driven search overviews. Reddit is aiming to convert its 170 million weekly active users into daily users, with a goal of 1 billion global daily active users. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: REG - Kingswood Holdings - Additional Debt Facility
data.description:
RNS Number : 8034A Kingswood Holdings Limited 14 March 2025  

Kingswood Holdings Limited 

("Kingswood", the "Company" or the "Group")

Additional Debt Facility

Further to the announcement on 12 March 2025 relating to the Company's immediate funding needs and the subsequent announcement that HSQ Investment Limited ("HSQ"), a wholly owned indirect subsidiary of funds managed and/or advised by Pollen Street Capital Limited ("Pollen Street"), has acquired all of the Kingswood Shares owned by KPI Nominees Ltd, Kingswood is pleased to announce an additional £4.8m debt facility (the "Facility Agreement") to provide the Company with the capital it needs to meet its immediate deferred consideration obligations.

The headline terms of the Facility Agreement are:

-       £4.8m facility;

-       Repayment date of earlier of 29 October 2030 or date of an exit or partial exit; and

-       Interest rate of 12%, paid at maturity.

Kingswood is entering into the Facility Agreement with an affiliate of HSQ Lindsey McMurray and Duncan Gerard are Non-Executive Directors of the Company and employees of Pollen Street Capital Limited ("Pollen Street") an affiliate of HSQ. Accordingly, entering into the Facility Agreement constitutes a related party transaction under Rule 13 of the AIM Rules for Companies.

As such, the Directors of the Company (other than Lindsey McMurray and Duncan Gerard) consider, having consulted with Cavendish Capital Market Limited, the Company's Nominated Adviser, that the terms of the Facility Agreement are fair and reasonable insofar as the Company's shareholders are concerned.

data.title: Toronto Stocks Rally; NFI Group Soars on 4Q Beat, New Bus Contract
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Stocks trading in Toronto were up in midday trading on Friday following Thursday's selloff. Most sectors advanced led by manufacturing, tech and process industries stocks. Communications was the only sector where stocks moved lower.

Canada's S&P/TSX Composite Index rose 1.3% to 24523.65, and the blue-chip S&P/TSX 60 was also up 1.3% to 1472.77.

Wholesale trade picked up in January, rising 1.2% from the previous month, driven by a second consecutive month of higher motor vehicle sales, according to data released Friday.

Shares of NFI Group climbed 23% to 13.66 Canadian dollars ($9.45) after the company reported better-than-expected fourth quarter results, and said that it won a contract to provide 170 clean-diesel transit buses to York Region Transit in Ontario.

Other market movers:

Athabasca Oil plans to renew its share repurchase program to buy back up to 10% of its stock in the course of a one-year period. Shares rose 0.8% to C$4.98.

Calfrac Well Services said that its chief executive, Pat Powell, has resigned after three years in the role. Shares for the oilfield services company rose 5.1% to C$3.69.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Bulgarian stock indices end week mixed, turnover stays low
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), March 14 (SeeNews) - The blue-chip SOFIX index of the Bulgarian Stock Exchange (BSE) ended the trading week 0.04% lower at 896.14 points, while the remaining four indices closed evenly split, bourse data showed.

The benchmark index was pressed by a 0.96% fall in Bulgarian Real Estate Fund REIT [BUL:BREF] to 4.14 levs and a 0.31% slip in Advance Terrafund REIT [BUL:ATER] to 3.21 levs.

Bulgarian Real Estate Fund and Advance Terrafund also constrained the BGREIT, which tracks seven real estate investment trusts, by 0.27% to 214.69 points.

The BEAMX index, which tracks the ten most highly capitalised growth companies, dropped 1.05% to 90.85 points, following an 8.33% slump in MFG Invest [BUL:MFG] to 2.20 levs.

In contrast, the BGBX40, which tracks the 40 most traded shares on the Sofia bourse, added 0.20% to 171.84 points. It was lifted by a 13.07% surge in diversified group Synergon Holding [BUL:SNRG] to 0.995 levs and a 4.84% leap in diversified group Zarneni Hrani Bulgaria [BUL:ZHBG] to 0.13 levs.

Hydraulic equipment manufacturer M+S Hydraulic [BUL:MSH], which grew by 4.37% to 9.55 levs, was the biggest gainer in the BGTR30. The index, in which companies with a free float of at least 10% have equal weight, went up 0.34% to 867.97 points.

Bulgarian Real Estate Fund was the most liquid stock of the day, generating 91,417 levs ($50,896/46,741 euro) as 22,144 shares changed hands.

Total turnover on the main market of the Sofia bourse declined to 382,235 levs on Friday from 468,889 levs on Thursday.

(1 euro = 1.95583 levs)

data.title: Italy's DiaSorin ex-Covid revenue in line with guidance
data.description:
Italian medical diagnostics group DiaSorin on Friday reported a 3% rise in 2024 revenue, citing dampened demand for its products with a milder Flu season compared to the previous year.

The group's 2024 revenue increased 3% year-on-year at constant exchange rates to 1.18 billion euros ($1.28 billion), while revenue was up 7% when excluding its COVID-related business, in line with guidance.

The group proposed a dividend of 1.20 euro per share.

Shares were 2.6% lower after the results.

data.title: Power Corp. Maintained at Market Perform at BMO Capital Markets; Price Target Raised to C$52.00
data.description:
BMO Capital Markets on Friday reiterated its market-perform rating on the shares of Power Corp. of Canada while raising its price target on the shares of the holding company to C$52.00 from C$49 on higher contributions from its companies.

"Target increases to $52 from $49, reflecting increased GWO/IGM targets post their respective Q4/24 results. Q4/24E adjusted EPS increase to $1.71 (consensus $1.66) from $1.70, reflecting modestly higher Q4/24 GWO/IGM/GBLB contributions, partially offset by negative impact of estimated consolidation adjustment (model refinement). Incorporating new estimates for modestly negative impact of consolidation adjustments, partially offset by recently increased GWO/IGM estimates, we modestly lower 2025E adjusted EPS to $5.42 from $5.47 (consensus $5.27), and introduce 2026E adjusted EPS of $5.98," analyst Tom MacKinnon.

data.title: Patagonia Gold Provides Update on the Development of the Calcatreu Project
data.description:
Patagonia Gold on Friday provided an update on the financing of its Calcatreu project in Argentina as Black River Mine Inc. agreed to acquire up to US$40 million of preferred shares in a company subsidiary.

Black River will acquire up to 40-million preferred shares of Patagonia Gold Canada which holds the project through its holdings of Minera Calcatreu SAU.

The investor is a newly formed company made up of a consortium of investors and controlled by Carlos Miguens, the managing director of Chile-based Aviron Investments.

The company expects to use proceeds to fund the development of the project and to pay fees and expenses incurred in the financing.

The company's shares were last seen unchanged at $0.035 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1214 ET - DocuSign's initial fiscal 2026 revenue outlook calls for sales to rise 5%, which strikes UBS analysts as a bit light, even if it is intended to be conservative, they say in a research note. The analysts' expectations stemmed from prior commentary from management that hinted at marginal demand improvement, as well as better-than-expected results in its fiscal 4Q. Despite touts from the e-signiture company that its AI-powered agreement-management platform is ramping, the offering doesn't appear to be boosting its outlook by much, the analysts write. The bank maintains its neutral rating and lowers its price target 10%, to $90. Shares rise 18% following an upbeat 4Q. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0358 ET - The stock market selloff is mainly driven by uncertainty around valuations, rather than growth concerns, Danske Bank's Sofie Pedersen says in a note. The U.S.'s largest tech stocks of Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, and Tesla--known as the magnificent 7--led declines on Thursday together with defensive real estate stocks, Pedersen says. In contrast, cyclical materials and financials were relative outperformers. "We interpret this as markets making it clear that it is not economic growth that is yet driving the selloff but uncertainty with valuation in focus rather than earnings," Danske says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1930 ET - The Nasdaq 100 is down 13% from its high, and the S&P 500 and S&P/ASX 200 is down 10.1%, so it could be time to reset expectations on where markets could go over the next few weeks to months, says Jessica Amir, market strategist at Moomoo Australia. The current magnitude of selling and shorting is just like it was in 2018 and 2022, and investors are yet to see company earnings expectations be materially lowered, she adds. It seems a lot of investment banks' buy-the-dip calls are fading, given no one knows what's ahead, Amir says. Huge sell orders from institutions in Nasdaq 100 stocks such as Nvidia and Tesla are evident. No one will win in this trade war, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1819 ET - Ulta lost market share in the beauty industry in 2024 for the first time as it faces tighter competition, Chief Executive Kecia Steelman says on an analyst call. The beauty retailer has struggled to keep up with lower-priced competitors and e-commerce companies like Amazon, which have gained greater share among younger customers who prefer to shop online. Ulta has added a wide array of fulfillment choices, including online shopping and same-day delivery, Steelman says, but those have resulted in both increased engagement and execution challenges. "In store presentation and guest experience today are not as strong as we would like," she says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1440 ET - The big question Adobe is facing? Whether it will be a winner or loser in the artificial intelligence boom, according to Quilter Cheviot's Ben Barringer. The analyst says Adobe is generating $125m in revenues from AI related activities. "On its own that is fine, and the business does expect that to double next year, however, for a company that generates north of $23bn in revenue, it remains [a] pittance," Barringer says. Adobe has long dominated the design software space, but that could be at risk if it can't keep up with AI trends and cedes market share to rival Canva, the analyst adds. Given the potential for market disruption, "you would have to say it is more likely to be a loser," Barringer says. Adobe sinks 13%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1349 ET - Mattel inked a licensing agreement with Warner Brothers Discovery last month, regaining the rights to develop toys based on DC characters such as Batman and Superman. The partnership is important, CEO Ynon Kreiz says at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference, because "it's among the upper echelon of the brands in the industry overall." The company intends to roll out a broad range of products starting in the second half of 2026, ranging from kids toys to high-end collectables aimed at adults. Kreiz says he expects the deal to meaningfully contribute to business: "It will be an important part of our portfolio and one that we believe we can elevate from its current level and take it to new heights," he adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1134 ET - Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says in a letter to shareholders accompanying the annual report that "we have put ourselves on the path to generating mid-teens returns through the cycle." Solomon says that running the firm more efficiently will help the company achieve its goals. He says Goldman will also lean on AI, including a developer copilot coding assistant and a natural-language GS AI assistant. "Throughout 2025, we intend to continue increasing the use of these tools in day-to-day workflows," Solomon writes. "These efficiencies will allow us to further invest in growth and improve the client experience over time," he says.(connor.hart@wsj.com)

1058 ET - Business leaders want more clarity from President Donald Trump's administration, which has hit the ground running with a burst of activity that has a wide range of potential policy outcomes, Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says in a letter to shareholders. "While policy uncertainty is to be expected within the first few months of any administration, it's important that policy positions become clearer so that businesses are able to make the decisions they need for longer-term planning and investment," he writes. Solomon adds that many of the CEOs he speaks with are currently evaluating the potential impacts of the administration's actions on their top and bottom lines: "As a result, we are seeing some of our corporate clients acting more cautiously until they have more clarity," he says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1048 ET - The ultimate direction of policies enacted under President Trump's new administration remains unclear, Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says in a letter to shareholders. "I expect that there will be many aspects that are supportive of growth, and others that could be less so," he writes. On one hand, there was a meaningful shift in CEO sentiment last fall following Trump's win that prompted increased appetite for deal-making, given the expected change in the regulatory environment, Solomon says. But on the other hand, discussion around tariffs "may weigh on corporate sentiment, and the impact on bottom lines could be significant," he writes. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1028 ET - The European market is lagging behind the U.S. market, though Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says in a letter to shareholders that Europe could accelerate its growth by easing up on regulations. "When we speak with leaders from the region, we hear a renewed sense of urgency to unlock the forces of dynamism and innovation," he writes. "My hope is that Europe's leaders have the public support and political will to make the necessary structural reforms to increase growth." (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1025 ET - Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon says in a letter to shareholders released with Goldman's Annual Report that financial market participants continue to recognize the competitiveness of the U.S. economy and the opportunities for sustained growth. However, he notes that recent market volatility is presenting a challenge. "Global growth has been hampered by inflation, an escalation in potential tariffs, and the toll of geopolitical tensions and prolonged conflicts across multiple regions," he writes. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0959 ET - UniCredit's decision to consider a longer time frame for a possible takeover of Commerzbank is welcome in light of its bid for Banco BPM at home, Equita says in a note. Today, the Italian lender gained ECB approval to raise its stake in its German peer to 29.9%. UniCredit said that taking a decision on whether to move forward with a potential acquisition would go beyond the end of 2025. This is positive as it would allow UniCredit time to integrate Banco BPM if that deal goes through, analyst Andrea Lisi writes. Once UniCredit gets approval to convert the part of Commerzbank's stake it holds through derivatives into physical shares, it should be able to impact the German bank's governance and explore possible partnerships, with Greece's Alpha Bank for example, he adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0748 ET - Ashmore Group now has a favorable risk-reward profile thanks to slower asset outflows, shifts in investors' allocation and attractive valuation, UBS says in a research note. After five years of outflows at the London-listed asset manager, which focuses on emerging markets, saw a turnaround with flows improving considerably in 2024 despite the strong dollar after the U.S. election, analysts write. "As investors reduce their U.S. overweights, we expect institutional investor allocations to EM debt and EM strategies to increase after hitting multi-decade lows last year," they add. The broker raises its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and its target price to 180 pence from 175 pence. Shares jump 8.4% to 162 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

BBVA's Chair Carlos Torres Vila was speaking at an industry event in Madrid. "Shareholders Should Decide on European Bank M&A, BBVA CEO Says — Market Talk," at 1127 GMT, misstated Vila's position at the bank.

0725 ET - As M&A returns to the European banking sector, it is important that shareholders are the ones who decide on the consolidation processes as this aligns with a well-functioning capital market, BBVA's Chair Carlos Torres Vila says at an industry event in Madrid. Spain's BBVA is waiting for the competition regulator's decision on its hostile takeover bid for peer Sabadell before putting the offer to shareholders. The nature of the all-share bid means that the premium initially offered has now become a discount. Europe needs larger and more competitive banks to support the continent's economic growth and innovation, the CEO says, adding that banking consolidation should be a strategic priority for the EU. "Before tackling cross-border mergers, it is essential to consolidate strong domestic banks." (elena.vardon@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 07:40 a.m. ET to clarify BBVA's Chair Carlos Torres Vila was speaking at an industry event in Madrid. An earlier article misstated Vila's position at the bank.

0359 ET - Swiss Life delivered solid 2024 results, but the company needs to beat expectations more strongly to justify its high valuation, Vontobel's Simon Foessmeier says in a research note. The Swiss insurer reported results that were broadly as expected, according to Vontobel. After-tax profit was in line with Vontobel's estimate, with fee and commission income slightly below expectations but fees better than the consensus, the analyst says. "While we are confident that management will deliver, the valuation multiples appear elevated. The firm's track record and the dividend--plus share buyback attraction--lead us to maintain our Hold rating," the analyst says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0358 ET - The stock market selloff is mainly driven by uncertainty around valuations, rather than growth concerns, Danske Bank's Sofie Pedersen says in a note. The U.S.'s largest tech stocks of Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Nvidia, and Tesla--known as the magnificent 7--led declines on Thursday together with defensive real estate stocks, Pedersen says. In contrast, cyclical materials and financials were relative outperformers. "We interpret this as markets making it clear that it is not economic growth that is yet driving the selloff but uncertainty with valuation in focus rather than earnings," Danske says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

2205 ET - Malaysia's equity market investors may remain defensive amid uncertainty over U.S. reciprocal tariff decisions on April 2, CIMB Securities analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia say in a note. Large foreign outflows, driven by risk aversion and weak 4Q 2024 corporate earnings, have pressured the KLCI, they note. The index is 8.6% lower year-to-date at 1500.65. Foreign shareholding stands at 19.6% as of end-February, just above the record low of 19.4%, they flag. Catalysts such as easing trade tensions, a stronger ringgit, improved corporate earnings, or government initiatives are needed to drive a rebound, they add. CIMB highlights Genting Malaysia, Sime Darby, Malayan Banking, Gamuda, and RHB Bank as key picks for their large-cap status and high dividend yields. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0951 ET - Crude futures are hovering in positive territory as U.S. stocks open higher and the market sees less chance of a quick ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine, although prospects of an end to the Russia/Ukraine war are still seen bearish for crude. A peace deal "would likely allow Russia to export oil and natural gas sanction free," Robert Yawger at Mizuho says in a note, adding that Russia would likely produce above its OPEC+ quota if there was demand, and "probably move quickly to start the export of natural gas into the EU." WTI and Brent are up 0.2% at $66.70 and $70.03 a barrel, respectively.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0556 ET - Enel's guidance for 2025 contained no surprises, since it was in line with targets presented in November, Stifel analysts write in a research note. The Rome-based energy company reported its 2024 results late Thursday and said it is targeting ordinary earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization of between 22.9 billion and 23.1 billion euros this year compared with the 22.8 billion euros it posted for 2024. Meanwhile, net ordinary income is expected to slip to between 6.7 billion and 6.9 billion euros from 7.1 billion euros. Shares trade down 0.6% at 6.95 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0551 ET - Enel's integrated business was resilient in 2024 and helped push its full-year results to the top of its guidance range, Jefferies analysts write in a note. The Rome-based energy company reported Thursday earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of 22.8 billion euros which was in line with the company's consensus, they write. Net income rose to 7.1 billion euros and beat the company consensus by 2.7%. Enel's earnings profile is strong and has high visibility despite a challenging market and geopolitical environment, they add. Shares trade flat at 6.99 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2157 ET - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2157 ET - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1934 ET - While Boss Energy is raising its exposure to TSX-listed junior Laramide Resources at a relatively low cost, UBS analysts say they'd prefer the Australian uranium miner's focus remain on its flagship Honeymoon project. In a note, the analysts say they understand Boss's intentions in buying more of Laramide but remain cautious given a moratorium on uranium mining in Australia's Queensland state, where Laramide is developing the Westmoreland project. Boss Thursday said it agreed to buy 9% more of Laramide, to take its stake to more than 18%. Laramide "holds the view that uranium mining will again be allowed within the state in short order," say the analysts, while highlighting that uranium has not been produced in Queensland since 1982. UBS has a buy rating and A$3.20 target on Boss. The stock is down 0.5% at A$2.20. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1809 ET - AGL Energy loses a bear in Barrenjoey following its review of default retail electricity market offers for FY 2026 in several states. Barrenjoey raises AGL to neutral, from underweight, as the offers suggest a better-than-expected outcome. "Our AGL forecasts in FY 2026 are now 5% above consensus EPS, and we suspect the support for greater cost recovery in non-Victorian states (and improved margin outlook) should support sentiment through to FY26 profit guidance in August," analyst Dale Koenders says. Still, Barrenjoey remains concerned about the outlook for AGL's earnings in FY 2028 when gas and coal contracts reset. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1805 ET - Jefferies approves of uranium miner Boss Energy's M&A ambitions. Boss Energy has bought another 9% of Laramide Resources, taking its stake in the owner of the undeveloped Westmoreland uranium project in Australia to 18.4%. That gives Boss a blocking stake on Westmoreland, and another project in the U.S., plus potential upside if they are developed, analyst Daniel Roden says. Acquiring the stake in Laramide follows previous deals with Eclipse Metals and enCore Energy. "Current transactions appear to be rational, modest and value accretive, while Honeymoon appears to be ramping up well," says Jefferies, referring to Boss's flagship uranium mine in South Australia. It retains a buy call on Boss. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1735 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is ready to start the day in the red after another rough session on Wall Street, where tariff threats continue to jolt markets. ASX futures are down by roughly 0.2% ahead of the open, suggesting the benchmark index will add to three consecutive days of losses. The ASX 200 ended Thursday 0.5% lower, at its lowest level since August. Gold futures notched a record high overnight, a tailwind for Australia's gold-mining stocks. Iron ore also rose, while oil futures fell. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1519 ET - Oil futures are ground down by another flare-up in the tariff saga as President Trump threatens 200% duties on European wine and other alcoholic beverages in response to the EU's retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. The tariff back-and-forth hitting equities has filtered into the oil market, along with concerns about the longer-term impact on economic growth and demand. That has coincided with talks for a ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine war, which could lead to additional Russian oil on the market. The IEA cited trade tensions in cutting its 2025 oil demand growth forecast to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d. WTI settles down 1.7% at $66.55 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.5% to $69.88 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

While commodities have been buffeted by tariff threats and soft economic data, fears of a major global slowdown are overdone, the analysts reckon. They revise their commodity forecasts, boosting their price estimates for gold but paring for metallurgical coal and lithium. 2240 ET - The planned acquisition of Radius Recycling by a U.S. unit of Toyota Tsusho at a roughly 115% premium looks positive for ASX-listed metals recycler Sims, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart. The Radius deal represents an EV/Ebitda multiple of 7.4X on 2027 estimates and a trailing three-year multiple of 8.3X, based on Visible Alpha consensus, says McTaggart. "We see this as a positive read-across for SGM with SGM trading on FY27 EV/Ebitda of 3.4X," he says. Citi has a neutral rating and A$15.50 target on Sims. The stock is up 4.7% at A$14.45. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2159 ET - Chinese shares are higher in early trade, supported by consumer and insurance stocks. Investors await China's February economic data due Monday, including industrial output and retail sales. Gainers in the session include Yonghui Superstores, which is up 1.7%, Kweichow Moutai up 0.9%, China Life Insurance adding 0.6% and Ping An Insurance 1.1% higher. Steel stocks are broadly lower, with Baoshan Iron & Steel down 1.6%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index is 0.3% higher at 3369.64, the Shenzhen Composite Index is up 0.1% and the ChiNext Price Index rises 0.7%. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

Depreciation was higher-than-anticipated, but net debt was as expected, say the analysts. They continue to expect Liontown will meet 2H FY25 guidance. Liontown is up 0.4% at A$0.6175. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1628 ET - Trade wars weigh down on U.S. stocks, pushing the S&P 500 into correction territory, off 10% from its February 19 closing high of 6,144.15, following the latest Trump threat: a 200% tariff on E.U. alcoholic beverages. Treasury yields fall amid the risk-off mode, despite mild producer inflation. Consumer discretionary and communication services drive losses among S&P sectors. American Eagle Outfitters declines 4.1% as investors worry about the company's ability to navigate slowing demand. Gold prices rise and miner Newmont's shares gain 4.6%. Nasdaq falls 2% to 17303 points and the S&P 500 drops 1.4% to 5522. DJIA is the only of the three major indexes not in correction territory yet, after losing 537 points, or 1.3%, to 40814. DJIA is now off 9% from its December 4, 2024 closing high of 45,014.04. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1308 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 0.2% at $9,770.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.1% at $2,702 a ton. Copper prices remain up 0.8% on week on tariff concerns, a weaker U.S. dollar and smelter output cuts in China, SP Angel analysts say in a note. Bullish calls continue to escalate on copper, as the physical market is expected to tighten outside the U.S. through May and June as the U.S. imports metal ahead of potential tariffs, SP Angel says. Copper smelter Tongling Nonferrous Metals has also cut its processing of concentrate by around 10% on weak smelting fees, analysts write. Fees have been ticking lower since the large-scale rollout of smelter capacity from China, Indonesia and Congo, SP Angel adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1324 GMT - German stocks rise, with defense, infrastructure and renewables companies among the biggest winners, after the country's likely future coalition reached a resolution with the Green party for an increase in fiscal spending. Shares in arms maker Rheinmetall jump 6% and top Germany's DAX blue-chip index, followed by building-materials supplier Heidelberg Materials and energy-equipment maker Siemens Energy, up 4.7% and 3.9%. Smaller defense companies Renk and Hensoldt jump 8% and 5.5%. In the MDAX index of mid-sized companies, IT group Bechtle, engineering firm Bilfinger and wind-turbine maker Nordex all trade more than 6% higher. Friedrich Merz, the man in line to be the country's next chancellor, said his Christian Democratic Union party and likely coalition party Social Democrats reached a tentative deal with the Greens over a financial package. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1240 GMT - Porsche has a long to-do list after it made a large reset to margin ambitions, Bernstein analysts write. Porsche's radically lower 2025 EBIT guidance range of 10%-12% came as it also set out a new mid-term guidance range of 15-17%. A year ago, CEO Oliver Blume was assuring the market its guidance of 15%-17% for 2024 was only transitional, and the company would return to its 17%-19% target in 2025. Porsche needs to stabilize China, restructure its operations, mitigate supply chain fragility and recalibrate its product plan towards stronger combustion engine and hybrid demand, Bernstein adds. The bank lowers its price target to 57 euros from 64 euros and keeps its market perform rating. Shares fall 0.7% to 53.80 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1139 GMT - BMW's 2025 guidance came in below consensus expectations, with the midpoint of the automotive margin at 6%, versus consensus of 6.4%, RBC Capital Markets' Tom Narayan writes. "This could be incorporating conservatism as we have been seeing across autos this earnings season however, especially considering macro uncertainty and the potential impacts of tariffs." The fourth-quarter automotive EBIT of 1.87 billion euros was in line with the 1.89 billion euros expected by consensus, driven by a 5.5% margin versus consensus at 5.3%. Automotive unit sales came in at 697,000 units versus consensus at 695,000 units. Revenue per unit was 48,879 euros versus consensus 50,584 euros. Shares rise 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1130 GMT - BMW's automotive margins are guided in the 5%-7% range, in line with Morgan Stanley's expectation but below the market consensus' expected 6%-8% range, in a still weak transition year, analysts at the bank say. BMW delivered on full-year reviewed targets and for 2025 expects a slight increase in automobile deliveries with a light uptick of battery electric vehicles share and flat EBIT margin. Fourth-quarter earnings are below expectations and the bank expects a negative investor reaction. Shares are 0.9% higher on the day having fallen around 4.3% in early trade. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1127 GMT - BMW has delivered a cautious outlook with fourth-quarter results that missed expectations, Stifel analyst Daniel Schwarz writes. The company is guiding for slight volume growth this year with an automotive EBIT margin of 5%-7% versus consensus at 6.9%. "We believe most investors expected EBIT margin guidance of 6%-8% for the automobiles division, i.e. this should be a negative surprise." Fourth-quarter revenues were a significant 13% miss while the automobiles EBIT was 6.6% below consensus, he adds. Shares rise 1.1%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1006 GMT - Daimler Truck's fourth-quarter result reflected improved profitability at the company's Trucks North America and better-than-expected margins at its Mercedes-Benz Trucks segment, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. Daimler's Trucks North America margin came in at 12.3% compared with consensus at 11.4% and a slight improvement compared with 12.1% in 3Q, the analysts say. Mercedes-Benz achieved an 8.1% margin, compared with expectations of 6.1%, Morgan Stanley says. Daimler is confident the lower end of its guidance for this year is achievable, the analysts say. The company guides for 2025 industrial business revenue of between 52 billion and 54 billion euros, adjusted EBIT growth of 5% to 15% with a 8% to 10% margin. Shares trade 1.4% higher to 39.52 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0943 GMT - Daimler Truck's guidance for 5% to 15% adjusted EBIT growth this year implies about 5.1 billion euros at the midpoint, around 2% ahead of consensus expectations, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. The company's guidance ranges for North American and European unit sales suggests a decline compared to 2024 in both markets at the midpoint, the analysts say. "In these market conditions, the expected EBIT generation is a strong result," Jefferies says. It adds that the company's fourth-quarter sales exceeded consensus expectations by 2%. Shares trade around 1% higher to 39.29 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - BMW investors will focus on the German carmaker's auto EBIT margin guidance of 5%-7%, BMW being the first car manufacturer to take into account the impact of U.S. tariffs in its outlook, Bernstein says in a note. This includes duties up to Wednesday, which account for roughly one percentage point of BMW's auto margin. "Were it not for these, the guidance would have been 6%-8%, which compares to what we reckon to be a buy-side consensus of 6%-7%," Bernstein analysts say. BMW shares trade 4.1% lower at 78.94 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0523 GMT - HMM may be less affected than its local peers by potential U.S. regulations on China, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. The South Korean shipping company operates a fleet of 125 vessels globally, and only 17 of them are Chinese-built ones, possibly subject to U.S. sanctions, Hwang says. If the U.S. were to hit Chinese vessels with high additional port fees, HMM could sell Chinese-built ships, as U.S. shipping routes account for 40% of its revenue, he adds. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price by 47% to KRW25,000. Shares are 0.2% higher at KRW20,650. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0353 GMT - HD Hyundai Heavy Industries is likely to become a key beneficiary of potential U.S. regulations on rival Chinese shipyards, Nomura analyst Eon Hwang writes in a note. The South Korean shipbuilder may utilize more of its production facilities, boost its ship delivery by 70% and double its profit margins if the U.S. regulates China, Hwang says. With the U.S. perceiving China's dominance in the global maritime industry as a threat, he expects the U.S. Trade Representative to move to impose additional port fees on Chinese-built vessels and operators. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises the target price by 21% to KRW400,000. Shares are 1.6% lower at KRW305,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - The Nasdaq 100 is down 13% from its high, and the S&P 500 and S&P/ASX 200 is down 10.1%, so it could be time to reset expectations on where markets could go over the next few weeks to months, says Jessica Amir, market strategist at Moomoo Australia. The current magnitude of selling and shorting is just like it was in 2018 and 2022, and investors are yet to see company earnings expectations be materially lowered, she adds. It seems a lot of investment banks' buy-the-dip calls are fading, given no one knows what's ahead, Amir says. Huge sell orders from institutions in Nasdaq 100 stocks such as Nvidia and Tesla are evident. No one will win in this trade war, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

1812 GMT - While Transat A.T. is taking steps to improve margins, macro headwinds could hamper its progress, according to National Bank of Canada in a report. Analyst Cameron Doerksen says that yields should improve in 2025 for the Canada-based airline and tour operator, and its Elevation Program aimed at maximizing long-term profitable growth is also a positive. But the current economic climate for Canada is less than stellar. "There are growing macroeconomic threats that could impact demand for travel in Canada," Doerksen says. Canada currently faces headwinds from escalating tariffs with the US which could hold back economic growth and domestic travel. Moreover, Doerksen says he remains "wary on the stock pending an expected comprehensive refinancing plan that could have negative implications for equity holders." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: NASA and SpaceX set for mission to swap out stuck astronauts
data.description:
Key points:
  • Crew-10 to swap out astronauts stuck on space station
  • Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams have been on ISS for nine months
  • SpaceX sets faster pace for NASA pre-launch check process
  • Wilmore and Williams could return to Earth March 19

NASA and SpaceX hope on Friday to launch a long-awaited crewed rocket that will let them bring home U.S. astronauts Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams, stuck on the International Space Station for nine months.

SpaceX and the U.S. space agency had planned to launch a replacement crew of four astronauts from Florida on Wednesday, a mission called Crew-10, but a last-minute problem with the rocket's ground systems forced a delay.

NASA on Thursday said SpaceX had resolved the issue - flushing a suspected pocket of air out of a hydraulic clamp arm - and that the weather was 95% percent favorable for a Friday launch.

Slated for liftoff at 7:03 p.m. ET Friday (2330 GMT), the Crew-10 will reach the ISS on Saturday night.

Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams, two veteran NASA astronauts and U.S. Navy test pilots, became the first humans to test-fly Boeing's Starliner spacecraft to the ISS.

But problems with Starliner's propulsion system during the flight forced an extension of their planned eight-day stay as NASA deemed it too risky for them to fly home on the craft, which returned to Earth empty.

The mission has also become entangled in politics as President Donald Trump and his adviser Elon Musk, SpaceX's CEO, say without evidence that former president Joe Biden left the astronauts on the station for political reasons.

"We came prepared to stay long, even though we planned to stay short," Wilmore said, adding that he did not believe NASA's decision to keep them on the ISS until Crew-10's arrival had been affected by politics.

"That's what your nation's human spaceflight program's all about," he said, "planning for unknown, unexpected contingencies. And we did that."

NASA says they have had to remain on the ISS to maintain its minimum staffing level.

Having seen their mission turn into a normal NASA rotation to the ISS, Wilmore and Williams have been doing scientific research and conducting routine maintenance with the other astronauts.

"UNUSUAL" FLIGHT PREPARATION PROCESS

Trump and Musk's demand for an earlier return was an unusual intervention, and NASA brought forward the Crew-10 mission from March 26, swapping a delayed SpaceX capsule for one that would be ready sooner.

The pressure from Musk and Trump has hung over a NASA preparation and safety process that normally follows a well-defined course.

NASA's Commercial Crew Program manager Steve Stich said SpaceX's "rapid pace of operations" had required NASA to change some of the ways it verifies flight safety.

The agency had to address some "late breaking" issues, NASA space operations chief Ken Bowersox told reporters, including investigating a fuel leak on a recent SpaceX Falcon 9 launch and deterioration of a coating on some of the Dragon crew capsule's thrusters.

Bowersox said it was hard for NASA to keep up with SpaceX: "We're not quite as agile as they are, but we're working well together."

When the new crew arrives aboard the station, Wilmore, Williams and two others - NASA astronaut Nick Hague and Russian cosmonaut Aleksandr Gorbunov - can return to Earth in a capsule that has been attached to the station since September, as part of the prior Crew-9 mission.

If Crew-10 launches as planned on Friday, it will dock to the ISS at 11:30 p.m. ET Saturday (0330 GMT Sunday), followed by a traditional handover ceremony that will allow for the Crew-9 crew's departure on March 19.

data.title: Bayer wins Brazil injunction in soy patent row with farmers
data.description:
Germany's Bayer has won an injunction to suspend a court ruling that gave Brazilian soybean farmers the right to reimbursement for royalties paid for expired patents of genetically modified soy seeds, according to a farmer group and a statement sent by the company to stakeholders this week.

The injunction prevents the Mato Grosso state lower court ruling from taking immediate effect, farmer lobby Aprosoja-MT said late on Thursday.

Bayer did not immediately reply when asked for comment.

On Wednesday, Bayer sent a note to stakeholders, a copy of which was obtained by Reuters, explaining that the company had successfully got the December 2024 ruling suspended until the Mato Grosso court of appeals reviews the case.

"This outcome preserves the current status quo, ensuring that the core principles of our business model... remain unchanged while the appeal is pending," Bayer told stakeholders, according to the note.

The company, which acquired U.S.-based Monsanto in 2016, has faced multiple legal challenges to the patents linked to its Intacta RR2 PRO technology in Brazil, the world's biggest soybean producer and exporter. The widely popular GMO seed combines tolerance to the herbicide glyphosate and protection against caterpillars.

The injunction is the latest twist in a protracted court battle in which the crop science firm and Brazilian farmers have clashed.

An estimated 4.5 billion reais ($785.30 million) in the form of "judicial guarantees" have been provided by Bayer while two lawsuits are ongoing, said Sidney de Souza, Aprosoja-MT's lawyer, on Friday.

In 2017, Mato Grosso farmers sued Monsanto in federal court seeking to cancel Intacta's patent protections. A final decision is pending, he said.

In 2023, Brazil's Supreme Court ruled that patent rights could not be extended beyond 20 years, cutting the duration of certain Intacta patents. That ruling affected 1.3 billion reais in Intacta royalties that had been paid by local farmers.

"The judge applied the Supreme Court's decision in our case, corrected the patent terms and ordered the return of unduly paid royalties," Souza said of the Mato Grosso ruling now suspended by the injunction.

($1 = 5.7303 reais)

data.title: USW National Director Marty Warren congratulates Prime Minister Mark Carney, calls for immediate action on worker protections, industrial policy and trade
data.description:
TORONTO, March 14, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — United Steelworkers union Canadian National Director Marty Warren issued the following statement:

“On behalf of the 225,000 members of the United Steelworkers union across Canada, I want to congratulate Mark Carney on becoming the 24th Prime Minister of Canada. Workers across the country are looking to this newly appointed government to stand up for them, defend their jobs and take real action to strengthen our economy.

Canada is in the middle of an escalating trade war that is threatening thousands of good jobs and entire communities. The latest U.S. tariffs on steel and aluminum are just one piece of a broader crisis, with unfair trade practices and economic uncertainty putting our jobs and the industries that depend on them at risk. We need leadership that will take real action – not just words.

The federal government must immediately strengthen support for affected workers by fixing Employment Insurance (EI) and introducing direct wage subsidies with job guarantees to keep people employed. Canada also needs bold investment in domestic manufacturing and infrastructure to secure long-term industrial stability. At the same time, the government must implement a “Buy Canadian” strategy to ensure public investment is used to buy Canadian-made manufactured goods and to support Canadian jobs. The government must also expand the application of section 53 of the Customs Tariff to stop foreign dumping and unfair competition.

USW members have been on the front lines of this fight. We will work with any government that is serious about protecting Canadian jobs and industries – but we won’t accept half-measures. The time for action is now. Prime Minister Carney has an opportunity to prove that his government is on the side of workers. Steelworkers are ready to work with him to deliver real solutions, but we will not hesitate to hold this government accountable if it fails to act.”

About the United Steelworkers union:

The USW represents 225,000 members in nearly every economic sector across Canada and is the largest private-sector union in North America, with 850,000 members in Canada, the United States and the Caribbean.

Each year, thousands of workers choose to join the USW because of the union’s strong track record in creating healthier, safer and more respectful workplaces and negotiating better working conditions and fairer compensation – including good wages, benefits and pensions.

For more information, please contact:

Shannon Devine, USW Communications, sdevine@usw.ca, 416-938-4402

A photo accompanying this announcement is available at https://www.globenewswire.com/NewsRoom/AttachmentNg/bd264c96-523e-496f-8597-1a81a7570b1e
